title: The lighthouse keeper's last winter
summary: When the last manned lighthouse in the Hebrides was automated, the keeper kept writing in the logbook anyway. Nobody asked him to stop.
body:
The Ardnamurchan lighthouse, on the western tip of mainland Scotland, was automated in 1988. The Northern Lighthouse Board removed the last keeper that summer. His name was Donald Macleod. He had worked there for twenty-two years. The light itself did not change. It still flashes white every twenty seconds, just as it had done since 1849.

What is unusual is the logbook. By tradition, every manned lighthouse kept a daily logbook of weather, sea state, passing ships, and any unusual sights. After 1988 there was no longer any reason to keep one. The machine does not need to write down what it sees. However, Donald Macleod, who had moved to a small cottage two miles inland, returned to the lighthouse every morning for the next fifteen years and wrote one page.

He was not paid for this. The Board knew about it and chose not to interfere. He had his own key, which had never been collected. The pages of his post-retirement logbook are now held at the National Library of Scotland. There are about five and a half thousand of them.

The entries are short. 'March 4, 1994. Force 6 west. Fishing boat Anna II passing at 06:40, low in the water, probably a good catch.' 'November 19, 1997. Snow on Mull. No traffic. The light is steady.' Occasionally there is a personal note: 'My wife in hospital this week. I came anyway.'

I asked his daughter, who lives in Oban, what he thought he was doing. She said her father did not consider it strange. 'He had been told the lighthouse no longer needed a witness. He disagreed.' She added that he had read somewhere — she did not remember where — that a place which is observed behaves differently from one which is not. He thought a coast that had been watched for nearly a hundred and forty years should not, suddenly, stop being watched.

The logbook stops on 14 February 2003. He died that April. His daughter found the last page in his coat pocket. It reads: 'Calm. Cold. The light is unchanged.'

In fact the light is still unchanged. It flashes every twenty seconds. Nobody has written in a logbook there since. A volunteer group has, however, recently asked the Board for permission to start again. The Board has not yet decided.

annotations.meaningVi: đã chuyển đến (trước thời điểm quá khứ)
không còn lý do nào — cụm phủ định nhấn mạnh sự chấm dứt của một hoạt động bắt buộc
một nơi được quan sát — cụm danh từ dùng mệnh đề quan hệ; quan điểm của người canh đèn: việc quan sát làm thay đổi chính nơi được quan sát
được nhiều cá / chuyến đánh bắt thuận lợi — idiom hàng hải; 'catch' (danh từ) = lượng cá bắt được trong một chuyến

grammarNotes.title: Past perfect vs past simple for layered past
grammarNotes.bodyVi: B1 dùng past perfect (had + V3) để chỉ một hành động đã xảy ra TRƯỚC một mốc quá khứ khác. Trong bài: 'Donald Macleod, who had moved to a small cottage two miles inland, returned to the lighthouse...' — anh đã chuyển nhà (past perfect) TRƯỚC khi tiếp tục đến đèn biển (past simple). 'He had been told the lighthouse no longer needed a witness. He disagreed.' — anh đã được nói (sớm hơn) trước khi tỏ ra bất đồng (sau).
grammarNotes.bodyEn: B1 uses past perfect (had + V3) to mark an action that happened BEFORE another past moment. In the passage: 'Donald Macleod, who had moved to a small cottage two miles inland, returned to the lighthouse...' — the move (past perfect) came BEFORE the daily return (past simple). 'He had been told the lighthouse no longer needed a witness. He disagreed.' — being told (earlier) came before disagreeing (later).

translationVi:
Đèn biển Ardnamurchan, ở mũi cực tây của Scotland đại lục, được tự động hoá năm 1988. Hội đồng Đèn biển miền Bắc đưa người canh cuối cùng đi mùa hè đó. Tên ông là Donald Macleod. Ông đã làm ở đó hai mươi hai năm. Bản thân ngọn đèn không đổi. Nó vẫn chớp trắng mỗi hai mươi giây, đúng như từ năm 1849.

Điều bất thường là cuốn nhật ký. Theo truyền thống, mỗi đèn biển có người trông đều ghi nhật ký hằng ngày về thời tiết, tình trạng biển, tàu thuyền qua lại, và bất kỳ điều gì khác lạ. Sau 1988 không còn lý do gì để ghi. Máy không cần viết ra cái nó thấy. Tuy nhiên, Donald Macleod, người đã chuyển về một ngôi nhà nhỏ cách đó hai dặm vào trong, vẫn quay lại đèn biển mỗi sáng suốt mười lăm năm tiếp theo và viết một trang.

Ông không được trả công. Hội đồng biết và chọn không can thiệp. Ông có chìa khoá riêng, chưa từng bị thu lại. Các trang nhật ký sau-nghỉ-hưu của ông giờ được giữ ở Thư viện Quốc gia Scotland. Có khoảng năm nghìn rưỡi trang.

Các dòng ghi rất ngắn. 'Ngày 4 tháng 3, 1994. Gió cấp 6 hướng tây. Tàu cá Anna II đi qua lúc 06:40, chìm sâu trong nước, chắc là được nhiều cá.' 'Ngày 19 tháng 11, 1997. Tuyết trên đảo Mull. Không có tàu qua. Đèn ổn định.' Đôi khi có một dòng cá nhân: 'Tuần này vợ tôi nằm viện. Tôi vẫn đến.'

Tôi hỏi con gái ông, sống ở Oban, ông nghĩ mình đang làm gì. Bà nói cha bà không thấy điều đó là lạ. 'Người ta bảo ông là đèn biển không cần nhân chứng nữa. Ông không đồng ý.' Bà nói thêm rằng ông đã đọc ở đâu đó — bà không nhớ ở đâu — rằng một nơi được quan sát thì cư xử khác với một nơi không được quan sát. Ông cho rằng một bờ biển đã được canh gần một trăm bốn mươi năm thì không nên, đột nhiên, ngừng được canh.

Nhật ký dừng vào ngày 14 tháng 2, 2003. Ông mất tháng Tư năm đó. Con gái ông tìm thấy trang cuối trong túi áo khoác cha. Nó ghi: 'Lặng. Lạnh. Đèn không đổi.'

Trên thực tế đèn vẫn không đổi. Nó chớp mỗi hai mươi giây. Không ai viết vào sổ ở đó kể từ đó. Tuy nhiên, gần đây một nhóm tình nguyện đã xin Hội đồng cho phép bắt đầu lại. Hội đồng chưa quyết định.

questions.prompt: When was the lighthouse automated and who was the last keeper?
options: 1988; Donald Macleod, who had worked there for 22 years

questions.prompt: How long has the light flashed every 20 seconds?
What did Macleod do after retirement?
options: Returned every morning for 15 years and wrote one page in a logbook

questions.prompt: How did the Lighthouse Board respond?
options: Knew about it and chose not to interfere; never collected his key

questions.prompt: Where are his pages now kept?
options: The National Library of Scotland; about 5,500 pages

questions.prompt: Which is an example of a personal note in his logbook?
options: 'My wife in hospital this week. I came anyway.'

questions.prompt: How did his daughter explain his behaviour?
options: He had been told the lighthouse no longer needed a witness — and he disagreed
He was forced by the Board

questions.prompt: What idea, picked up somewhere in his reading, guided him?
options: A place that is observed behaves differently from one that is not
A volunteer group has asked the Board for permission to start the logbook again; no decision yet
His daughter took over the post
Even when a machine takes over a task, the human act of witnessing a place may still matter; a retired keeper kept a record nobody required, and his pages became a record nobody now ignores.
Volunteer labour should always replace paid work.
Old keepers should be paid pensions.
questions.explanation: The writer's framing across the whole essay.
questions.hint: Look at the last two paragraphs.

cloze.template:
The Ardnamurchan lighthouse, on the western tip of mainland Scotland, was {{b1}} in 1988. The Northern Lighthouse Board removed the last keeper that summer. His name was Donald Macleod. He had worked there for twenty-two years. The light itself did not change. It still flashes white every twenty seconds, just as it had done since {{b2}}.

What is unusual is the {{b3}}. By tradition, every manned lighthouse kept a daily logbook of weather, sea state, passing ships, and any unusual sights. After 1988 there was no longer any reason to keep one. The machine does not need to write down what it sees. However, Donald Macleod, who had moved to a small cottage two miles inland, returned to the lighthouse every morning for the next {{b4}} years and wrote one page.

He was not paid for this. The Board knew about it and chose not to {{b5}}. He had his own key, which had never been collected. The pages of his post-retirement logbook are now held at the National Library of Scotland. There are about five and a half thousand of them.

The entries are short. 'March 4, 1994. Force 6 west. Fishing boat Anna II passing at 06:40, low in the water, probably a good catch.' 'November 19, 1997. Snow on Mull. No traffic. The light is steady.' Occasionally there is a personal note: 'My wife in hospital this week. I came anyway.'

I asked his daughter, who lives in Oban, what he thought he was doing. She said her father did not consider it strange. 'He had been told the lighthouse no longer needed a {{b6}}. He disagreed.' She added that he had read somewhere — she did not remember where — that a place which is {{b7}} behaves differently from one which is not. He thought a coast that had been watched for nearly a hundred and forty years should not, suddenly, stop being watched.

The logbook stops on 14 February {{b8}}. He died that April. His daughter found the last page in his coat pocket. It reads: 'Calm. Cold. The light is {{b9}}.'

In fact the light is still unchanged. It flashes every twenty seconds. Nobody has written in a logbook there since. A {{b10}} group has, however, recently asked the Board for {{b11}} to start again. The Board has not yet decided.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Macleod believed that 'a place which is observed behaves differently from one which is not.' Is there a place in your own life — a street, a building, a stretch of water — that has stopped being watched in the way it used to be? What changed when the watching stopped?
